metadata.title: No, political actors do not get their message into the news: an analysis of the effect of interest group press releases

abstract: Significant progress has been made in mapping the media efforts as well as the media coverage of policy advocates such as interest groups, think tanks and private corporations. However, less attention has been devoted to connecting media efforts to media coverage. In this research note, we therefore ask: To what extent do the media-targeted activities of policy advocates actually influence the news? In a first analytic step, we use three different methods to assess whether interest groups influence the news: automated identification based on anti-plagiarism software, computer-assisted topic modelling and manual word search. Based on the results of this first analysis, we use manual word search to identify possible media effects of a larger sample of all press releases issued by five major Danish interest groups in 2018. We conclude that the press releases issued by interest groups have very little-and most often no-direct influence on the news.

sections.paper_id: Binderkrantz et al. - 2023 - No, political actors do not get their message into the news an analysis of the effect of interest g
paragraphs: Media coverage is generally seen as a significant stepping stone towards political influence (Strömbäck and Esser 2014;Trapp and Laursen 2017). As a result, policy advocates routinely try to affect who gets into the news, what issues are on the media agenda, and how stories are framed. In the last two decades, significant progress has been made in mapping the media efforts as well as the media coverage of policy advocates such as interest groups, think tanks and private corporations (see for example: Aizenberg and Hanegraaff 2019;Bernhagen 2011;Beyers 2004;Binderkrantz et al. 2017). However, little attention has been devoted to connecting media efforts to media coverage. In this research note, we therefore ask: To what extent do the media-targeted activities of policy advocates actually influence the news?

Linking media effort to news coverage is crucial for understanding the dynamics that shape media coverage in modern democracies. First, it speaks to the issue of the balance between reporters and their sources: To what degree are news stories the result of the independent efforts of reporters, and to what degree do they reflect the input reporters receive from political parties and policy advocates? Second, it allows analysis of whether some types of political actors are able to routinely push their issues into the news, while others gain little leverage from their strategic activities. Reflecting this, party scholars increasingly compare the content of press releases from political parties to news stories (Meyer et al. 2017;Haselmayer et al. 2017Haselmayer et al. , 2019;;Hopmann et al. 2012;Ketelaars and Sevenans 2020).

By contrast, only few studies of interest groups connect groups' strategic efforts to media coverage, and when they do, they adopt a very general perspective on the issue (Grömping 2019;Oehmer 2017). In this research note, we take a first step towards filling this gap by analysing the effect of interest group press releases on news coverage. The analysis draws on a new dataset with press releases from major Danish interest groups and news stories in national newspapers. This allows us to establish a direct link between press releases carefully written by interest groups to get their message through and actual news stories.

In a first analytical step, we use three different methods to assess whether interest groups influence the news: automated identification based on anti-plagiarism software, computer-assisted topic modelling and word search. This allows us to cast the net widely in identifying possible effects of press releases that may include direct quotes, stories on the topic of the press releases and brief mentioning of the name of the group and/or its position. In a second analytical step, we extend the scope of analysis to all press releases issued by five major Danish interest group in a full year. Relying on the results of the first analytical step, we base this analysis on a word search strategy combined with manual coding of the results. We conclude that the press releases issued by interest groups have very little-and most often no-direct influence on the news. Even though interest groups dedicate vast resources to pressdirected work, it is hard to find traces of this in news reporting.
sections.text:
Media coverage is generally seen as a significant stepping stone towards political influence (Strömbäck and Esser 2014;Trapp and Laursen 2017). As a result, policy advocates routinely try to affect who gets into the news, what issues are on the media agenda, and how stories are framed. In the last two decades, significant progress has been made in mapping the media efforts as well as the media coverage of policy advocates such as interest groups, think tanks and private corporations (see for example: Aizenberg and Hanegraaff 2019;Bernhagen 2011;Beyers 2004;Binderkrantz et al. 2017). However, little attention has been devoted to connecting media efforts to media coverage. In this research note, we therefore ask: To what extent do the media-targeted activities of policy advocates actually influence the news?

Linking media effort to news coverage is crucial for understanding the dynamics that shape media coverage in modern democracies. First, it speaks to the issue of the balance between reporters and their sources: To what degree are news stories the result of the independent efforts of reporters, and to what degree do they reflect the input reporters receive from political parties and policy advocates? Second, it allows analysis of whether some types of political actors are able to routinely push their issues into the news, while others gain little leverage from their strategic activities. Reflecting this, party scholars increasingly compare the content of press releases from political parties to news stories (Meyer et al. 2017;Haselmayer et al. 2017Haselmayer et al. , 2019;;Hopmann et al. 2012;Ketelaars and Sevenans 2020).

By contrast, only few studies of interest groups connect groups' strategic efforts to media coverage, and when they do, they adopt a very general perspective on the issue (Grömping 2019;Oehmer 2017). In this research note, we take a first step towards filling this gap by analysing the effect of interest group press releases on news coverage. The analysis draws on a new dataset with press releases from major Danish interest groups and news stories in national newspapers. This allows us to establish a direct link between press releases carefully written by interest groups to get their message through and actual news stories.

In a first analytical step, we use three different methods to assess whether interest groups influence the news: automated identification based on anti-plagiarism software, computer-assisted topic modelling and word search. This allows us to cast the net widely in identifying possible effects of press releases that may include direct quotes, stories on the topic of the press releases and brief mentioning of the name of the group and/or its position. In a second analytical step, we extend the scope of analysis to all press releases issued by five major Danish interest group in a full year. Relying on the results of the first analytical step, we base this analysis on a word search strategy combined with manual coding of the results. We conclude that the press releases issued by interest groups have very little-and most often no-direct influence on the news. Even though interest groups dedicate vast resources to pressdirected work, it is hard to find traces of this in news reporting.

sections.paper_id: Binderkrantz et al. - 2023 - No, political actors do not get their message into the news an analysis of the effect of interest g
sections.header_raw: Literature review: a missing link between media effort and news coverage
sections.header_norm: literature review a missing link between media effort and news coverage
paragraphs: This research note takes as its point of departure two highly robust findings in the research literature. First, political actors such as organised interests and parties spend vast resources on media-directed efforts-issuing press releases, contacting reporters or staging events to attract media attention. Moreover, media strategies are not a fall back option for actors with little or no access to politics. On the contrary, those interest groups who are most active in insider politics are also highly active in their media efforts (Beyers 2004;Binderkrantz 2005;Dür and Mateo 2016).

Second, political actors appear frequently in news stories, and much scholarly attention has therefore been devoted to assessing the extent of bias and diversity in interest group appearances in the news media (Bernhagen and Trani 2012;De Bruycker and Beyers 2015;Thrall 2006;Tiffen et al. 2013). From these studies, a number of findings stand out as particularly consistent. First, media access is highly skewed with a small number of groups receiving a disproportionate level of attention. Second, interest group resources-for example finances or staff-affect their level of media access (Binderkrantz et al. 2015;Grömping 2019;Oehmer 2017).

Surprisingly, little attention has been devoted to connecting these two findings: Do media-targeted activities of interest groups actually influence the news? A few have approached the issue in an indirect manner. Binderkrantz and Pedersen (2019) for example rely on interest group surveys to link the strategies groups engage in and their self-reported influence on the political agenda. Only two studies provide direct analysis of the link between media effort and news reports. Oehmer (2017) finds a positive correlation between the number of press releases issued by a group and the level of media attention the group receives. Moreover, she finds that groups who are more successful in adapting the content of press releases to media logics are more successful. In a comparative analysis covering 118 countries, Grömping (2019) finds group media efforts-as reported in a group survey-to be a strong predictor of the media access of human rights groups.

While these two studies represent an important step forward, their focus is at the aggregate level, linking overall media efforts by groups and their media appearance. They therefore do not tell us whether interest groups are able to affect the news directly by engaging in media-directed tactics, or whether there is a more indirect connection between the overall media efforts of groups and their aggregate level of media access.

This research note takes as its point of departure two highly robust findings in the research literature. First, political actors such as organised interests and parties spend vast resources on media-directed efforts-issuing press releases, contacting reporters or staging events to attract media attention. On the contrary, those interest groups who are most active in insider politics are also highly active in their media efforts (Beyers 2004;Binderkrantz 2005;Dür and Mateo 2016).

In a comparative analysis covering 118 countries, Grömping (2019) finds group media efforts-as reported in a group survey-to be a strong predictor of the media access of human rights groups.

sections.paper_id: Binderkrantz et al. - 2023 - No, political actors do not get their message into the news an analysis of the effect of interest g
paragraphs: In this research note, we aim to gauge the extent to which specific media-directed tactics have the intended effect by leading to one or more news stories. We do so by investigating the impact of press releases from five of the most important Danish interest groups in a selection of newspapers in Denmark. Press releases are ideal for testing interest group influence on the media. They are short, to the point and carefully written by the interest group to make the media report on them and their viewpoints. Moreover, they are easy to produce and therefore topical in the sense that they can be made as an important event unfolds. Oxfam can, to exemplify, issue a highly specific press release to call for an end to poverty during a draught in Africa and yet another to help after an earthquake in Himalaya. Press releases are also attractive to newspapers. Given tighter reporting budgets in the digital age, newspaper editors and journalists may gladly use the information-quotes and arguments-contained in press releases (Grimmer 2013: 34;Meyer et al. 2017: 285). Indeed, the US congressional press releases are sometimes run almost verbatim in local newspapers (Grimmer 2013: 35;Meyer et al. 2017: 285).

Denmark is an appropriate testing ground because of its plural media market in which news outlets have weak party affiliations. This is important, because strong media-party ties might constrain the interest groups so that business associations can only get into right-leaning papers and vice versa for trade unions. Yet, such constraints are only weakly present in the Danish case. In general, Denmark is a typical European democracy with a diverse media landscape with a variety of quality and tabloid newspapers as well as intense political competition over the mass media agenda (e.g. Green-Pedersen and Stubager 2010; Binderkrantz et al. 2017). Our findings should, in this sense, be representative of other European countries.

We select our interest groups to approach a 'most likely' study. Hence, we select five big, resourceful interest groups: the Confederation of Danish Employers, the Association of Danish Regions, Local Government Denmark, the Danish Agriculture and Food Council and the Danish Confederation of Trade Unions. We first collected all press releases published by these groups in 2018. The key empirical challenge is to link the press releases to the content of news articles. We use a dataset of all news articles published in 2018 in five of the largest Danish newspapers: Berlingske, Børsen, Ekstra Bladet, Jyllands-Posten and Politiken (a dataset of 214,186 news articles). For each press release, we create a subset of news articles published within the first three days after the press release is published, assuming that if it has not entered the news by then, it is unlike to do so later. Conversely, if we allow for too long a window there is a risk of spuriously claiming a link where there is none, especially when relying on thematic overlap as part of our approach (cf. below).

In a first analytic step, we use three different methods to trawl the database for a subset of 100 press releases, namely an anti-plagiarism software, topic modelling and word search. These methods are increasingly used in research on political advocacy, influence and communication (on plagiarism software, see, e.g.

McKay 2018; Haeder and Yackee 2015, 2020; Røed 2022; on topic modelling, see, e.g. Sagarzazu and Klüver 2017; Ketelaars and Sevenans 2020; Grimmer 2013, on word search and manual content coding, see, e.g. Aizenberg and Hanegraff 2019; Bruycker and Beijers 2015, Junk and Rasmussen 2018; for related methods, see, e.g. Bernhagen and Trani 2012;Bernhagen 2011;Grimmer 2013;Meyer et al. 2017;Haselmeyer et al. 2017Haselmeyer et al. , 2019)). Our second analytical step includes all the press releases issued by the five groups in 2018 and based on the results of the first analysis, we use word search in combination with manual coding to identify possible effects of the press releases.

We use the anti-plagiarism software WCopyfind version 4.1.5 to identify any 'matches' between each press release and the news articles in their corresponding subset. In this context, 'matches' are defined as the occurrence of the same four or more words in two documents, i.e. a press release and a news article. We allow, however, for one word to be different. We chose the low number of four words and the allowance of a one-word difference to find as many matches as possible. This method found 960 potential cases of news articles referencing press releases.

For the topic modelling approach, we use Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), a type of unsupervised natural language processing that can find patterns in text documents through statistics based on the placement of each word compared to other words (Blei et al. 2003). We performed the analysis in R. 1 First, our pre-processing consists of removing numbers, punctuations and other symbols in the text to arrive at its shortest form and then removing stop words (e.g. 'or' and 'thus'). Afterwards, all words appearing in less than four different text files, i.e. the subset of news articles and the press release, are removed from the text files. Last, words consisting of only one letter are also removed. We conduct two sequences of topic modelling for each press release and its subset of news articles. The first narrows down the number of articles. The second is then applied to the narrowed subset of news articles, the result of which is used to categorise news articles as potentially referencing the press release.

The first sequence starts by looking for 13 topics within the news articles and the press release. If one topic makes up 80% of the words in the press release, the first step of the topic modelling process is complete. If not, the process continues until the 80% threshold is met, or the number of topics reaches 28.foot_2 If the threshold is not met, the final application is based on the number of topics where the press release had the highest score within one topic. The news articles, which are made up of 50% or more of the prominent topic of the press release, are used in the second step of the topic modelling process. In this step, the start number of topics is flexible. If the number of news articles is equal to or less than 39, then the start number of topics is 13, and if the number of articles is more than 39, then the start number of topics is 19. This is done because it is assumed that more news articles, all things being equal, lead to more topics. Initial testing showed that these two numbers of topics performed best. The threshold for when a topic is sufficiently prominent for a press release is 85%. This method found 147 potential cases for all 100 press releases.

The third and last method we used is word search. We performed this method by manually reading all 100 press releases, then noting two keywords for each press release. The name of the authoring interest group of each press release was also added as a third keyword. We then searched each subset of news articles for: (1) mentions of any of the three keywords per their corresponding press release or (2) mentioning of at least two of the keywords. Any article that mentioned one or more of the keywords/two keywords were subsequently categorised as a potential case of a news article that references a press release. These two methods found 6177 and 287 potential cases of news articles referencing press releases.

Each of our methods yields a pool of potential links, which we then manually code as having either an explicit link (i.e. a news article that directly mentions a press release) or a thematic overlap (i.e. a news article that addresses the same specific topic as the press release). The former is, on the one hand, hard proof that the interest group was able to affect the news media with its press release. They include examples where for example statistics from the press release are cited in the news stories or where the same direct quotes appear in both the press release and the news story. For example a news story quotes the chairperson of Danish Regions as stating that "the staff should probably be better at informing patients that a more critically ill patient has arrived in order to make those waiting aware that this has a legitimate reason". This exact quote also appears in a press release sent out by the Association of Danish Regions.

This approach may, on the other hand, overlook situations where reporters are inspired by the press release without directly mentioning it. We therefore also code for thematic overlap between the press release and the pool of articles. This includes for example news stories about a report issued by a governmental "stress panel" since this was also the subject of a press release from the Confederation of Danish Employers-even though the association is not quoted or mentioned in the news story. While the explicit link approach may be too restrictive, the thematic overlap approach may, of course, be too lenient. Yet, in combination, they should give us a clear picture of the range of influence we are dealing with.

By using these different methods to search for news stories written in response to press releases, we cast our net widely. This is important since we aim to provide as encompassing an analysis of the effects of press releases as possible. Moreover, we register not only articles that contain an explicit link to the press release, but also those with a thematic overlap. Figure 1 displays a first summary of the results. For each of the methods-anti-plagiarism (blue bars), topic modelling (red bars) and the two types of word search (brown bars)-the figure reports the total number of articles which we can confidently say either contains an explicit reference or a thematic overlap. For instance, the anti-plagiarism software yielded 960 articles that could potentially contain a link. Yet, of these articles only 10 actually did have an explicit reference to a press release (cf. the number on top of the left-most bar), or just north of 1% (cf. the number in the brackets on top of the left-most bar). Similarly, of the 960 articles with potential links just 62 actually contained a thematic overlap, equivalent to 6.5%.

Looking across the different methods, it becomes clear that the number of explicit links and thematic overlap is low. Word search for any of three keywords stands out as the method that generates the highest number of links, namely 18 articles with an explicit reference to a press release and 131 articles with a thematic overlap. While this version of word search yields the highest number of correctly identified links, it is interesting to see that this method is also quite ineffective because it entails casting the net very wide. The topic modelling and plagiarism approach performs better with respect to the percentages of correctly identified articles, but only identifies 4 and 10 articles with explicit link. The word search using at least two keywords is relatively effective in finding news stories with an explicit link (identifying 15 articles) and finds most of the stories with some level of thematic overlap. Moreover, it is the most efficient method with respect to the number of articles that needs to be manually coded in order to identify the relevant ones. Based on these results, we use this strategy for the second part of the analysis where all press releases issued in 2018 by the five interest groups are included.

This analysis includes a total of 356 press releases and for each of these we have repeated the steps described above, but relying only on a word search with at least two keywords as the criteria for including news stories in the set of potential hits. Panel A in Fig. 2 displays the distribution of articles with an explicit reference across press releases, while Panel B displays the distribution of articles with a thematic overlap. Of the 356 press releases, 311 have no explicit reference, 36 have one reference, eight have two references while only one press release has three explicit references. The distribution is slightly less skewed when it comes to thematic overlap. Here 227 press releases have an overlap with one or more articles, while in a handful of the cases the media gave substantial attention to the theme of the press release.

In addition, we compare the media effects of press releases issued by each of the five different group, to gauge if there are pronounced differences across them. For this sub-analysis reported in Fig. 3, we divide the articles that explicitly reference a press release/contain a thematic overlap with the number of press releases issued per group. This gives us an impression of the relative success rate of each of the five interest groups when issuing press releases. In terms of explicit references to the left in Fig. 3, Local Government Denmark and the Confederation of Employers are the most successful, while the Danish Confederation of Trade Unions and the Danish Agriculture and Food Council are the least successful, a picture roughly mirrored when looking at the thematic overlap to the right in Fig. 3. The biggest difference comes out on the measure of explicit references, where the most successful groups only need to issue four press releases for every mention in an article, whereas the least successful group needs to issue around four times as many press releases. This is, indeed, a marked difference, but in the context of a low success rate for all groups.
sections.text:
In this research note, we aim to gauge the extent to which specific media-directed tactics have the intended effect by leading to one or more news stories. We do so by investigating the impact of press releases from five of the most important Danish interest groups in a selection of newspapers in Denmark. Press releases are ideal for testing interest group influence on the media. They are short, to the point and carefully written by the interest group to make the media report on them and their viewpoints. Moreover, they are easy to produce and therefore topical in the sense that they can be made as an important event unfolds. Oxfam can, to exemplify, issue a highly specific press release to call for an end to poverty during a draught in Africa and yet another to help after an earthquake in Himalaya. Press releases are also attractive to newspapers. Given tighter reporting budgets in the digital age, newspaper editors and journalists may gladly use the information-quotes and arguments-contained in press releases (Grimmer 2013: 34;Meyer et al. 2017: 285). Indeed, the US congressional press releases are sometimes run almost verbatim in local newspapers (Grimmer 2013: 35;Meyer et al. 2017: 285).

Denmark is an appropriate testing ground because of its plural media market in which news outlets have weak party affiliations. Our findings should, in this sense, be representative of other European countries.

We first collected all press releases published by these groups in 2018. The key empirical challenge is to link the press releases to the content of news articles. We use a dataset of all news articles published in 2018 in five of the largest Danish newspapers: Berlingske, Børsen, Ekstra Bladet, Jyllands-Posten and Politiken (a dataset of 214,186 news articles). For each press release, we create a subset of news articles published within the first three days after the press release is published, assuming that if it has not entered the news by then, it is unlike to do so later. Conversely, if we allow for too long a window there is a risk of spuriously claiming a link where there is none, especially when relying on thematic overlap as part of our approach (cf. below).

In a first analytic step, we use three different methods to trawl the database for a subset of 100 press releases, namely an anti-plagiarism software, topic modelling and word search. These methods are increasingly used in research on political advocacy, influence and communication (on plagiarism software, see, e.g.

McKay 2018; Haeder and Yackee 2015, 2020; Røed 2022; on topic modelling, see, e.g. Sagarzazu and Klüver 2017; Ketelaars and Sevenans 2020; Grimmer 2013, on word search and manual content coding, see, e.g. Aizenberg and Hanegraff 2019; Bruycker and Beijers 2015, Junk and Rasmussen 2018; for related methods, see, e.g. Bernhagen and Trani 2012;Bernhagen 2011;Grimmer 2013;Meyer et al. 2017;Haselmeyer et al. 2017Haselmeyer et al. , 2019)). Our second analytical step includes all the press releases issued by the five groups in 2018 and based on the results of the first analysis, we use word search in combination with manual coding to identify possible effects of the press releases.

We use the anti-plagiarism software WCopyfind version 4.1.5 to identify any 'matches' between each press release and the news articles in their corresponding subset. In this context, 'matches' are defined as the occurrence of the same four or more words in two documents, i.e. a press release and a news article. We allow, however, for one word to be different. We chose the low number of four words and the allowance of a one-word difference to find as many matches as possible. This method found 960 potential cases of news articles referencing press releases.

For the topic modelling approach, we use Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), a type of unsupervised natural language processing that can find patterns in text documents through statistics based on the placement of each word compared to other words (Blei et al. 2003). We performed the analysis in R. 1 First, our pre-processing consists of removing numbers, punctuations and other symbols in the text to arrive at its shortest form and then removing stop words (e.g. 'or' and 'thus'). Afterwards, all words appearing in less than four different text files, i.e. the subset of news articles and the press release, are removed from the text files. Last, words consisting of only one letter are also removed. We conduct two sequences of topic modelling for each press release and its subset of news articles. The first narrows down the number of articles. The second is then applied to the narrowed subset of news articles, the result of which is used to categorise news articles as potentially referencing the press release.

The first sequence starts by looking for 13 topics within the news articles and the press release. If one topic makes up 80% of the words in the press release, the first step of the topic modelling process is complete. If not, the process continues until the 80% threshold is met, or the number of topics reaches 28.foot_2 If the threshold is not met, the final application is based on the number of topics where the press release had the highest score within one topic. The news articles, which are made up of 50% or more of the prominent topic of the press release, are used in the second step of the topic modelling process. In this step, the start number of topics is flexible. If the number of news articles is equal to or less than 39, then the start number of topics is 13, and if the number of articles is more than 39, then the start number of topics is 19. This is done because it is assumed that more news articles, all things being equal, lead to more topics. Initial testing showed that these two numbers of topics performed best. The threshold for when a topic is sufficiently prominent for a press release is 85%. This method found 147 potential cases for all 100 press releases.

The third and last method we used is word search. We performed this method by manually reading all 100 press releases, then noting two keywords for each press release. The name of the authoring interest group of each press release was also added as a third keyword. We then searched each subset of news articles for: (1) mentions of any of the three keywords per their corresponding press release or (2) mentioning of at least two of the keywords. Any article that mentioned one or more of the keywords/two keywords were subsequently categorised as a potential case of a news article that references a press release. These two methods found 6177 and 287 potential cases of news articles referencing press releases.

Each of our methods yields a pool of potential links, which we then manually code as having either an explicit link (i.e. a news article that directly mentions a press release) or a thematic overlap (i.e. a news article that addresses the same specific topic as the press release). The former is, on the one hand, hard proof that the interest group was able to affect the news media with its press release. They include examples where for example statistics from the press release are cited in the news stories or where the same direct quotes appear in both the press release and the news story. For example a news story quotes the chairperson of Danish Regions as stating that "the staff should probably be better at informing patients that a more critically ill patient has arrived in order to make those waiting aware that this has a legitimate reason". This exact quote also appears in a press release sent out by the Association of Danish Regions.

This approach may, on the other hand, overlook situations where reporters are inspired by the press release without directly mentioning it. We therefore also code for thematic overlap between the press release and the pool of articles. This includes for example news stories about a report issued by a governmental "stress panel" since this was also the subject of a press release from the Confederation of Danish Employers-even though the association is not quoted or mentioned in the news story. While the explicit link approach may be too restrictive, the thematic overlap approach may, of course, be too lenient. Yet, in combination, they should give us a clear picture of the range of influence we are dealing with.

By using these different methods to search for news stories written in response to press releases, we cast our net widely. This is important since we aim to provide as encompassing an analysis of the effects of press releases as possible. Moreover, we register not only articles that contain an explicit link to the press release, but also those with a thematic overlap. Figure 1 displays a first summary of the results. For each of the methods-anti-plagiarism (blue bars), topic modelling (red bars) and the two types of word search (brown bars)-the figure reports the total number of articles which we can confidently say either contains an explicit reference or a thematic overlap. For instance, the anti-plagiarism software yielded 960 articles that could potentially contain a link. Yet, of these articles only 10 actually did have an explicit reference to a press release (cf. the number on top of the left-most bar), or just north of 1% (cf. the number in the brackets on top of the left-most bar). Similarly, of the 960 articles with potential links just 62 actually contained a thematic overlap, equivalent to 6.5%.

Looking across the different methods, it becomes clear that the number of explicit links and thematic overlap is low. Word search for any of three keywords stands out as the method that generates the highest number of links, namely 18 articles with an explicit reference to a press release and 131 articles with a thematic overlap. While this version of word search yields the highest number of correctly identified links, it is interesting to see that this method is also quite ineffective because it entails casting the net very wide. The topic modelling and plagiarism approach performs better with respect to the percentages of correctly identified articles, but only identifies 4 and 10 articles with explicit link. The word search using at least two keywords is relatively effective in finding news stories with an explicit link (identifying 15 articles) and finds most of the stories with some level of thematic overlap. Moreover, it is the most efficient method with respect to the number of articles that needs to be manually coded in order to identify the relevant ones. Based on these results, we use this strategy for the second part of the analysis where all press releases issued in 2018 by the five interest groups are included.

This analysis includes a total of 356 press releases and for each of these we have repeated the steps described above, but relying only on a word search with at least two keywords as the criteria for including news stories in the set of potential hits. Panel A in Fig. 2 displays the distribution of articles with an explicit reference across press releases, while Panel B displays the distribution of articles with a thematic overlap. Of the 356 press releases, 311 have no explicit reference, 36 have one reference, eight have two references while only one press release has three explicit references. The distribution is slightly less skewed when it comes to thematic overlap. Here 227 press releases have an overlap with one or more articles, while in a handful of the cases the media gave substantial attention to the theme of the press release.

In addition, we compare the media effects of press releases issued by each of the five different group, to gauge if there are pronounced differences across them. For this sub-analysis reported in Fig. 3, we divide the articles that explicitly reference a press release/contain a thematic overlap with the number of press releases issued per group. This gives us an impression of the relative success rate of each of the five interest groups when issuing press releases. In terms of explicit references to the left in Fig. 3, Local Government Denmark and the Confederation of Employers are the most successful, while the Danish Confederation of Trade Unions and the Danish Agriculture and Food Council are the least successful, a picture roughly mirrored when looking at the thematic overlap to the right in Fig. 3. The biggest difference comes out on the measure of explicit references, where the most successful groups only need to issue four press releases for every mention in an article, whereas the least successful group needs to issue around four times as many press releases. This is, indeed, a marked difference, but in the context of a low success rate for all groups.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
In this research note, we aim to gauge the extent to which specific media-directed tactics have the intended effect by leading to one or more news stories. We do so by investigating the impact of press releases from five of the most important Danish interest groups in a selection of newspapers in Denmark. Press releases are ideal for testing interest group influence on the media. They are short, to the point and carefully written by the interest group to make the media report on them and their viewpoints. Moreover, they are easy to produce and therefore topical in the sense that they can be made as an important event unfolds. Oxfam can, to exemplify, issue a highly specific press release to call for an end to poverty during a draught in Africa and yet another to help after an earthquake in Himalaya. Press releases are also attractive to newspapers. Given tighter reporting budgets in the digital age, newspaper editors and journalists may gladly use the information-quotes and arguments-contained in press releases (Grimmer 2013: 34;Meyer et al. 2017: 285). Indeed, the US congressional press releases are sometimes run almost verbatim in local newspapers (Grimmer 2013: 35;Meyer et al. 2017: 285).

Denmark is an appropriate testing ground because of its plural media market in which news outlets have weak party affiliations. This is important, because strong media-party ties might constrain the interest groups so that business associations can only get into right-leaning papers and vice versa for trade unions. Yet, such constraints are only weakly present in the Danish case. In general, Denmark is a typical European democracy with a diverse media landscape with a variety of quality and tabloid newspapers as well as intense political competition over the mass media agenda (e.g. Green-Pedersen and Stubager 2010; Binderkrantz et al. 2017). Our findings should, in this sense, be representative of other European countries.

We select our interest groups to approach a 'most likely' study. Hence, we select five big, resourceful interest groups: the Confederation of Danish Employers, the Association of Danish Regions, Local Government Denmark, the Danish Agriculture and Food Council and the Danish Confederation of Trade Unions. We first collected all press releases published by these groups in 2018. The key empirical challenge is to link the press releases to the content of news articles. We use a dataset of all news articles published in 2018 in five of the largest Danish newspapers: Berlingske, Børsen, Ekstra Bladet, Jyllands-Posten and Politiken (a dataset of 214,186 news articles). For each press release, we create a subset of news articles published within the first three days after the press release is published, assuming that if it has not entered the news by then, it is unlike to do so later. Conversely, if we allow for too long a window there is a risk of spuriously claiming a link where there is none, especially when relying on thematic overlap as part of our approach (cf. below).

In a first analytic step, we use three different methods to trawl the database for a subset of 100 press releases, namely an anti-plagiarism software, topic modelling and word search. These methods are increasingly used in research on political advocacy, influence and communication (on plagiarism software, see, e.g.

McKay 2018; Haeder and Yackee 2015, 2020; Røed 2022; on topic modelling, see, e.g. Sagarzazu and Klüver 2017; Ketelaars and Sevenans 2020; Grimmer 2013, on word search and manual content coding, see, e.g. Aizenberg and Hanegraff 2019; Bruycker and Beijers 2015, Junk and Rasmussen 2018; for related methods, see, e.g. Bernhagen and Trani 2012;Bernhagen 2011;Grimmer 2013;Meyer et al. 2017;Haselmeyer et al. 2017Haselmeyer et al. , 2019)). Our second analytical step includes all the press releases issued by the five groups in 2018 and based on the results of the first analysis, we use word search in combination with manual coding to identify possible effects of the press releases.

We use the anti-plagiarism software WCopyfind version 4.1.5 to identify any 'matches' between each press release and the news articles in their corresponding subset. In this context, 'matches' are defined as the occurrence of the same four or more words in two documents, i.e. a press release and a news article. We allow, however, for one word to be different. We chose the low number of four words and the allowance of a one-word difference to find as many matches as possible. This method found 960 potential cases of news articles referencing press releases.

For the topic modelling approach, we use Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), a type of unsupervised natural language processing that can find patterns in text documents through statistics based on the placement of each word compared to other words (Blei et al. 2003). We performed the analysis in R. 1 First, our pre-processing consists of removing numbers, punctuations and other symbols in the text to arrive at its shortest form and then removing stop words (e.g. 'or' and 'thus'). Afterwards, all words appearing in less than four different text files, i.e. the subset of news articles and the press release, are removed from the text files. Last, words consisting of only one letter are also removed. We conduct two sequences of topic modelling for each press release and its subset of news articles. The first narrows down the number of articles. The second is then applied to the narrowed subset of news articles, the result of which is used to categorise news articles as potentially referencing the press release.

The first sequence starts by looking for 13 topics within the news articles and the press release. If one topic makes up 80% of the words in the press release, the first step of the topic modelling process is complete. If not, the process continues until the 80% threshold is met, or the number of topics reaches 28.foot_2 If the threshold is not met, the final application is based on the number of topics where the press release had the highest score within one topic. The news articles, which are made up of 50% or more of the prominent topic of the press release, are used in the second step of the topic modelling process. In this step, the start number of topics is flexible. If the number of news articles is equal to or less than 39, then the start number of topics is 13, and if the number of articles is more than 39, then the start number of topics is 19. The threshold for when a topic is sufficiently prominent for a press release is 85%. This method found 147 potential cases for all 100 press releases.

The third and last method we used is word search. We performed this method by manually reading all 100 press releases, then noting two keywords for each press release. The name of the authoring interest group of each press release was also added as a third keyword. We then searched each subset of news articles for: (1) mentions of any of the three keywords per their corresponding press release or (2) mentioning of at least two of the keywords. Each of our methods yields a pool of potential links, which we then manually code as having either an explicit link (i.e. a news article that directly mentions a press release) or a thematic overlap (i.e. a news article that addresses the same specific topic as the press release). They include examples where for example statistics from the press release are cited in the news stories or where the same direct quotes appear in both the press release and the news story. For example a news story quotes the chairperson of Danish Regions as stating that "the staff should probably be better at informing patients that a more critically ill patient has arrived in order to make those waiting aware that this has a legitimate reason". Yet, of these articles only 10 actually did have an explicit reference to a press release (cf. the number on top of the left-most bar), or just north of 1% (cf. the number in the brackets on top of the left-most bar). Similarly, of the 960 articles with potential links just 62 actually contained a thematic overlap, equivalent to 6.5%.

Moreover, it is the most efficient method with respect to the number of articles that needs to be manually coded in order to identify the relevant ones. In addition, we compare the media effects of press releases issued by each of the five different group, to gauge if there are pronounced differences across them. sections.paper_id: Binderkrantz et al. - 2023 - No, political actors do not get their message into the news an analysis of the effect of interest g
paragraphs: Our results paint a picture of relatively low rates of success when interest groups try to get media attention by issuing press releases. For most press releases, it is hard to find direct traces of them in the news media. Even when we took a rather inclusive focus on news stories with thematic overlap, we were not able to find a match for many of the press releases. This conclusion is corroborated by our very exhaustive search strategy utilising an anti-plagiarism search tool, automated topic modelling as well as a simple search for keywords in articles. It is also notable that we selected five of the major, resourceful interest groups in Denmark for the analysis which should increase the options that the media would react to the press releases. We did find meaningful differences across interest groups, with some being around four times as likely to see their press releases covered. Yet, even for the most successful groups, pickup rates were low and with only five groups included our results with respect to differences across groups must be regarded tentative.

We set out with an ambition to link the press-related work of interest groups to their appearance in the news (Grömping 2019;Oehmer 2017). Our analysis points to weak media effects of the press releases issued by interest groups. It is clear that reporters do not automatically include content from press releases in their news stories. On the contrary, many press releases are simply ignored. Potentially, it could Fig. 3 Distribution of news articles across interest organisations take a longer push of multiple press releases in the same direction to prompt media coverage (i.e. a nonlinear threshold effect). Our methods do not allow us to pick up such a process of slow diffusion of ideas. Still, we are fairly confident in concluding that the immediate impact of issuing press releases is limited.

Importantly, this does not necessarily mean that policy advocates are unable to affect the media. Press releases are just one of the tools in the repertoire of mediadirected tactics, and groups may for example have more leverage by approaching reporters directly. To the extent that direct contacts are effective in attracting media attention, it has implications for the options for less resourceful groups to gain media traction. While issuing press releases is a relatively inexpensive tactic, resources and existing networks are likely to be more crucial for maintaining such direct contacts. Our results thus speak to concerns about inequality in political representation and influence.

With respect to the balance between reporters and their sources, our results indicate that interest groups do not have the upper hand. Even the most prominent interest groups cannot simply issue a press release and expect reporters to react. Yet, these groups regularly appear in the news. As discussed above, this may be related to other types of media efforts than press releases. It could, however, also indicate that groups appear mainly upon invitation from the media-that is when reporters reach out to groups rather than the other way around. This is a channel that may be unrelated to press releases or other types of press efforts by groups, yet, it still allows interest groups a considerable footprint in the media. Any understanding of the media presence of interest groups thus needs to incorporate the logics guiding the media as they decide which sources to include in their news stories (Aizenberg and Hanegraaff 2019;Bernhagen 2011;Binderkrantz et al. 2017).

Even if other techniques may be more important, the number of press releases issued by interest groups indicates that this tool looms prominently in their toolbox. Why do they issue so many press releases if it does not bring them into the news? There may be three explanations. First, the interest groups sincerely want to get into the news and this will by definition never happen if they do not try. Interest groups thus issue press releases in the hope that at least some of them will be picked up by reporters. Second, the real targets of press releases may not be reporters, but rather group members and supporters. By issuing press releases, groups can show their members that they actively try to push member interests. Finally, most major interest groups have large professional secretariats. For communication units in groups, press releases may be a way of demonstrating their worth and thus upholding their budgets. In other words, interest groups may be prone to the same logics as other bureaucratic organisations. No matter the reason, we believe that our study calls for a more critical approach to studying interest group influence on the media.
sections.text:
Our results paint a picture of relatively low rates of success when interest groups try to get media attention by issuing press releases. For most press releases, it is hard to find direct traces of them in the news media. Even when we took a rather inclusive focus on news stories with thematic overlap, we were not able to find a match for many of the press releases. This conclusion is corroborated by our very exhaustive search strategy utilising an anti-plagiarism search tool, automated topic modelling as well as a simple search for keywords in articles. It is also notable that we selected five of the major, resourceful interest groups in Denmark for the analysis which should increase the options that the media would react to the press releases. We did find meaningful differences across interest groups, with some being around four times as likely to see their press releases covered. Yet, even for the most successful groups, pickup rates were low and with only five groups included our results with respect to differences across groups must be regarded tentative.

We set out with an ambition to link the press-related work of interest groups to their appearance in the news (Grömping 2019;Oehmer 2017). Our analysis points to weak media effects of the press releases issued by interest groups. It is clear that reporters do not automatically include content from press releases in their news stories. On the contrary, many press releases are simply ignored. Potentially, it could Fig. 3 Distribution of news articles across interest organisations take a longer push of multiple press releases in the same direction to prompt media coverage (i.e. a nonlinear threshold effect). Our methods do not allow us to pick up such a process of slow diffusion of ideas. Still, we are fairly confident in concluding that the immediate impact of issuing press releases is limited.

Importantly, this does not necessarily mean that policy advocates are unable to affect the media. Press releases are just one of the tools in the repertoire of mediadirected tactics, and groups may for example have more leverage by approaching reporters directly. To the extent that direct contacts are effective in attracting media attention, it has implications for the options for less resourceful groups to gain media traction. While issuing press releases is a relatively inexpensive tactic, resources and existing networks are likely to be more crucial for maintaining such direct contacts. Our results thus speak to concerns about inequality in political representation and influence.

With respect to the balance between reporters and their sources, our results indicate that interest groups do not have the upper hand. Even the most prominent interest groups cannot simply issue a press release and expect reporters to react. Yet, these groups regularly appear in the news. As discussed above, this may be related to other types of media efforts than press releases. It could, however, also indicate that groups appear mainly upon invitation from the media-that is when reporters reach out to groups rather than the other way around. This is a channel that may be unrelated to press releases or other types of press efforts by groups, yet, it still allows interest groups a considerable footprint in the media. Any understanding of the media presence of interest groups thus needs to incorporate the logics guiding the media as they decide which sources to include in their news stories (Aizenberg and Hanegraaff 2019;Bernhagen 2011;Binderkrantz et al. 2017).

Even if other techniques may be more important, the number of press releases issued by interest groups indicates that this tool looms prominently in their toolbox. Why do they issue so many press releases if it does not bring them into the news? There may be three explanations. First, the interest groups sincerely want to get into the news and this will by definition never happen if they do not try. Interest groups thus issue press releases in the hope that at least some of them will be picked up by reporters. Second, the real targets of press releases may not be reporters, but rather group members and supporters. By issuing press releases, groups can show their members that they actively try to push member interests. Finally, most major interest groups have large professional secretariats. For communication units in groups, press releases may be a way of demonstrating their worth and thus upholding their budgets. In other words, interest groups may be prone to the same logics as other bureaucratic organisations. No matter the reason, we believe that our study calls for a more critical approach to studying interest group influence on the media.

To the extent that direct contacts are effective in attracting media attention, it has implications for the options for less resourceful groups to gain media traction. While issuing press releases is a relatively inexpensive tactic, resources and existing networks are likely to be more crucial for maintaining such direct contacts. Our results thus speak to concerns about inequality in political representation and influence.

With respect to the balance between reporters and their sources, our results indicate that interest groups do not have the upper hand. Even the most prominent interest groups cannot simply issue a press release and expect reporters to react. Yet, these groups regularly appear in the news. As discussed above, this may be related to other types of media efforts than press releases. It could, however, also indicate that groups appear mainly upon invitation from the media-that is when reporters reach out to groups rather than the other way around. This is a channel that may be unrelated to press releases or other types of press efforts by groups, yet, it still allows interest groups a considerable footprint in the media. Any understanding of the media presence of interest groups thus needs to incorporate the logics guiding the media as they decide which sources to include in their news stories (Aizenberg and Hanegraaff 2019;Bernhagen 2011;Binderkrantz et al. 2017).

Even if other techniques may be more important, the number of press releases issued by interest groups indicates that this tool looms prominently in their toolbox. Why do they issue so many press releases if it does not bring them into the news? There may be three explanations. First, the interest groups sincerely want to get into the news and this will by definition never happen if they do not try. Interest groups thus issue press releases in the hope that at least some of them will be picked up by reporters. Second, the real targets of press releases may not be reporters, but rather group members and supporters. By issuing press releases, groups can show their members that they actively try to push member interests. Finally, most major interest groups have large professional secretariats. For communication units in groups, press releases may be a way of demonstrating their worth and thus upholding their budgets. In other words, interest groups may be prone to the same logics as other bureaucratic organisations. No matter the reason, we believe that our study calls for a more critical approach to studying interest group influence on the media.
sections.preview_head: Our results paint a picture of relatively low rates of success when interest groups try to get media attention by issuing press releases. For most press releases, it is hard to find direct traces of them in the news media. Even when we took a rather inclusive focus on news stories with thematic overlap, we were not able to find a match for many of the press releases.
sections.preview_tail: For communication units in groups, press releases may be a way of demonstrating their worth and thus upholding their budgets. In other words, interest groups may be prone to the same logics as other bureaucratic organisations. No matter the reason, we believe that our study calls for a more critical approach to studying interest group influence on the media.
